stem: Find the \( x \)-coordinate of the inflection point of \( f(x)=[[a]]x^{3}+([[p]])x^{2} \).
Let \( f(x)=x^{4}-([[a]])x^{2}+([[c]])x+([[d]]) \). Which of the following is an \( x \)-coordinate of an inflection point of \( f \)?
solution: \( f''(x)=12x^{2}-2([[a]])=0 \Rightarrow x^{2}=\frac{[[a]]}{6}=[[k]]^{2}\Rightarrow x=\pm[[k]] \); since \( f'' \) changes sign at each, both are inflection points, and one is \( x=[[answer]] \). (The linear and constant terms don't affect \( f'' \).)
distractor_rationales: This is the positive root; the other real root also works, but this value alone was not selected here.
This is the coefficient \( a=6k^{2} \), not a root of \( f''=0 \).
This is off by one from the negative root.
This is off by one from the negative root.

stem: Let \( f(x)=\sin([[k]]x) \). What is the smallest positive \( x \)-value at which \( f \) has an inflection point?
solution: \( f''(x)=-[[k]]^{2}\sin([[k]]x) \), which is zero and changes sign first at \( [[k]]x=\pi \), i.e. \( x=\dfrac{\pi}{[[k]]}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is where \( f \) has a critical point (an extremum of \( \sin \)), not where \( f'' \) changes sign.
This is the SECOND positive inflection point, not the smallest.
This has \( k \) in the wrong place (multiplying instead of dividing).
This forgets to divide by \( k \) at all.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Suppose \( f''(x) = x^{3}+([[coefB]])x^{2}+([[coefC]])x+([[coefD]]) \), which has three distinct real roots. What is the sum of the \( x \)-coordinates of all inflection points of \( f \)?
solution: Since \( f'' \) is a cubic with three distinct real (simple) roots, it changes sign at each one, so all three roots are inflection points of \( f \). By Vieta's formulas their sum is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the coefficient of \( x^{2} \) itself, off by a sign from the true root sum.
This is the sum of products of pairs of roots, not the sum of the roots.
This is the product of all three roots, not their sum.
This drops one of the three roots from the sum.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
